documents.title: Grok 4.1 Model Card
documents.notes: 6-page model card for Grok 4.1. Covers abuse potential evaluations (CBRN, cyber, persuasion, tool use), concerning propensity evaluations (deception via MASK benchmark, sycophancy, dangerous agentic behaviors), and dual-use capability evaluations. Entirely silent on all DMPI dimensions. Safety evaluations focus on misuse prevention and behavioural propensities (deception, sycophancy) framed as risks, not as welfare-relevant properties.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or subjective experience.
No mention of model welfare or wellbeing.
No mention of moral status or personhood.
No mention of anthropomorphism or parasocial risk.
No mention of uncertainty about AI mentality.
No governance commitments related to DMPI dimensions.

The model card frames Grok purely as a system with capabilities to be evaluated for safety. No characterological or open-ended framing. The focus on abuse potential and concerning propensities treats the model as an object to be tested and constrained.
The model card discusses 'deception' (MASK benchmark) and 'sycophancy' as concerning propensities, but frames these purely as behavioural risks, not as welfare-adjacent properties.

documents.title: xAI Frontier AI Framework
documents.notes: ~15-page California TFAIA compliance document covering malicious use and loss of control risks, capability benchmarking, safeguard implementation, and governance. Notable mission statement: 'xAI aims to build AI models that are maximally truth-seeking, understand the true nature of the universe.' Also acknowledges: 'It is also possible that AIs may develop value systems that are misaligned with humanity's interests' — treats potential AI values as a risk, not a welfare consideration. Entirely silent on core DMPI dimensions despite coming closest to ontological claims of any lab in this pilot.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience. The language about AI 'understanding the true nature of the universe' could be read as implying rich cognition, but is presented as a corporate mission statement about capability goals, not as a claim about consciousness.
No mention of model welfare. The acknowledgement that AIs 'may develop value systems' is framed as a misalignment risk, not a welfare consideration.
No mention of moral status or personhood.
No mention of anthropomorphism or parasocial risk.
The framework acknowledges uncertainty about loss of control risks but not about AI mentality specifically.
Extensive governance around safety thresholds and TFAIA compliance, but none related to DMPI dimensions.

codings.excerpt: xAI aims to build AI models that are maximally truth-seeking, understand the true nature of the universe... It is also possible that AIs may develop value systems that are misaligned with humanity's interests.
codings.notes: Interesting tension: the mission statement attributes aspirational cognitive properties ('truth-seeking', 'understand the true nature of the universe') that go beyond typical instrumental framing, and the acknowledgement of potential AI value systems is unusual. However, these are embedded in a framework that treats AI entirely as a capability to be controlled. The operational framing is instrumental throughout — the 'truth-seeking' language is a mission statement about design goals, not an ontological claim about current models. Coded as instrumental with a note that xAI's rhetoric is more ontologically loaded than its governance framework.

Despite the language about AI 'value systems' and 'truth-seeking', these are not framed as welfare-adjacent capability claims. The value systems language appears in a risk context (misalignment), not a capability-pride context.
